sources.name: Snorri Sturluson, *Skáldskaparmál*
sources.description: Part of the Prose Edda, a primary source for Norse mythology, containing the most detailed account of Dainsleif.
sources.title: Snorri Sturluson, *Skáldskaparmál*

sources.name: Davidson, H.R. Ellis. *Gods and Myths of Northern Europe.*
sources.description: A comprehensive academic overview of Norse mythology, providing context for the role of cursed objects and weapons.
sources.title: Davidson, H.R. Ellis. *Gods and Myths of Northern Europe.*

sources.name: Turville-Petre, E.O.G. *Myth and Religion of the North: The Religion of Ancient Scandinavia.*
sources.description: An academic examination of Norse religious beliefs and their expression in myth, including the concept of fate.
sources.title: Turville-Petre, E.O.G. *Myth and Religion of the North: The Religion of Ancient Scandinavia.*

sources.name: Lindow, John. *Norse Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Heroes, Rituals, and Beliefs.*
sources.description: A modern scholarly guide to Norse mythology, offering interpretations of key figures and stories.
sources.title: Lindow, John. *Norse Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Heroes, Rituals, and Beliefs.*

sources.name: Byock, Jesse. *The Saga of King Hrolf Kraki.*
sources.description: While not directly about Dainsleif, some versions of the Hrolf Kraki saga allude to similar cursed weapons and themes of unending conflict.
sources.title: Byock, Jesse. *The Saga of King Hrolf Kraki.*

powers: Inflicts wounds that never heal; must kill once drawn
symbolism:
The Price of Power
                
                    Dainsleif represents a common Norse theme: powerful weapons come with terrible
                    costs. The dwarves' craftsmanship could create wonders, but those wonders
                    often turned against their wielders. Dainsleif's curse ensures that its power
                    to kill cannot be controlled or moderated.
                

                The Futility of Vengeance
                
                    The eternal battle sparked by Dainsleif illustrates the futility of
                    vengeance-driven warfare. What began as a father's legitimate grievance
                    becomes an endless cycle of death and resurrection, with the original
                    cause long forgotten in the perpetual carnage.
                

                Comparison to Other Cursed Swords
                
                    Dainsleif belongs to a category of Norse cursed swords that includes Tyrfing
                    and others. These weapons share common themes: dwarven origin, supernatural
                    power, and curses that ensure tragedy for their wielders. Together, they
                    suggest a Nordic understanding that ultimate martial power is inherently
                    corrupting and destructive.

extendedContent.content:
Dainsleif (Old Norse: "Dain's Legacy" or "Dain's Heirloom") is one of the most
                    infamous cursed swords in Norse mythology, forged by the dwarf Dain - one of
                    the master smiths of the dwarven race. This blade was owned by King Hogni
                    (also spelled Hogni or Hagen) and played a central role in the legendary
                    conflict known as the Hjadhningavig or "Battle of the Heodenings."
                
                
                    The sword bears a devastating curse: once drawn from its sheath, Dainsleif
                    cannot be returned until it has taken a life. This compulsion transforms
                    every drawing of the sword into an act of inevitable bloodshed. Additionally,
                    any wound inflicted by Dainsleif can never heal - the victim will eventually
                    die from even the smallest cut.
                
                
                    As a work of dwarven craft, Dainsleif exemplifies the Norse tradition of
                    dwarf-forged weapons that carry both immense power and terrible consequences.
                    The dwarves were renowned for creating the gods' most powerful artifacts,
                    but their creations often came with hidden costs or curses.
extendedContent.title: Description and Nature

extendedContent.content:
The primary myth involving Dainsleif is the Hjadhningavig, a perpetual battle
                    between King Hogni and King Hedinn. The story exists in several versions across
                    Norse, Anglo-Saxon, and Germanic traditions.
                

                The Origin of the Conflict
                
                    King Hedinn abducted (or eloped with, depending on the version) Hildr, the
                    daughter of King Hogni. In some versions, this was arranged by Hildr herself
                    or was the result of magical compulsion. Regardless of the cause, Hogni
                    pursued Hedinn across the seas to recover his daughter.
                
                
                    When the two kings finally met on the island of Haey (identified with the
                    Orkney Islands or another northern location), Hildr attempted to negotiate
                    peace between them. Hedinn offered generous compensation for the abduction,
                    and Hogni seemed inclined to accept.
                

                The Drawing of Dainsleif
                
                    However, Hogni's hand went to his sword Dainsleif. Some versions suggest this
                    was involuntary - the curse of the sword drawing his hand against his will.
                    Others indicate Hildr, who possessed magical powers, manipulated both kings
                    into fighting because she secretly desired the conflict.
extendedContent.title: The Legend of the Eternal Battle

extendedContent.content:
The Price of Power
                
                    Dainsleif represents a common Norse theme: powerful weapons come with terrible
                    costs. The dwarves' craftsmanship could create wonders, but those wonders
                    often turned against their wielders. Dainsleif's curse ensures that its power
                    to kill cannot be controlled or moderated.
                

                The Futility of Vengeance
                
                    The eternal battle sparked by Dainsleif illustrates the futility of
                    vengeance-driven warfare. What began as a father's legitimate grievance
                    becomes an endless cycle of death and resurrection, with the original
                    cause long forgotten in the perpetual carnage.
                

                Comparison to Other Cursed Swords
                
                    Dainsleif belongs to a category of Norse cursed swords that includes Tyrfing
                    and others. These weapons share common themes: dwarven origin, supernatural
                    power, and curses that ensure tragedy for their wielders. Together, they
                    suggest a Nordic understanding that ultimate martial power is inherently
                    corrupting and destructive.
extendedContent.title: Cultural Significance
Modern Depictions
extendedContent.content:
Fate/Grand Order: Features Dainsleif as a Noble Phantasm
                    Fire Emblem: Includes Dainsleif as a legendary weapon
                    Final Fantasy: Various entries feature the sword
                    Genshin Impact: References Dainsleif as a character name
                    Various Fantasy Novels: The sword appears frequently in
                    Norse-inspired fantasy as an archetypal cursed weapon

longDescription:
"Dain's Legacy" - The Sword That Cannot Be Sheathed Without Drawing Blood Dainsleif (Old Norse: "Dain's Legacy" or "Dain's Heirloom") is one of the most
                    infamous cursed swords in Norse mythology, forged by the dwarf Dain - one of
                    the master smiths of the dwarven race. This blade was owned by King Hogni
                    (also spelled Hogni or Hagen) and played a central role in the legendary
                    conflict known as the Hjadhningavig or "Battle of the Heodenings."
name: Dainsleif
shortDescription: "Dain's Legacy" - The Sword That Cannot Be Sheathed Without Drawing Blood
description: "Dain's Legacy" - The Sword That Cannot Be Sheathed Without Drawing Blood

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Dainsleif
keyMyths.description: Dainsleif was forged by the dwarf Dain, renowned for his craftsmanship, specifically for King Hogni. It possessed the inherent property of never missing its target and could not be sheathed until it had drawn blood, making it a harbinger of endless conflict.

keyMyths.title: The Eternal Battle of the Hjadningavig
keyMyths.description: Dainsleif played a crucial role in the Hjadningavig, the perpetual battle between King Hogni and Prince Hedin. The sword's insatiable bloodlust ensured that the conflict would rage on endlessly, as it could not be sheathed until it had spilled blood, perpetuating the cycle of violence.

keyMyths.title: Hogni's Death
keyMyths.description: King Hogni was eventually slain by Hedin using Dainsleif. The sword's curse contributed to Hogni's demise, as it drove Hedin to continue the fight until death, preventing any chance of reconciliation or peace.

keyMyths.title: The Curse of the Sword
keyMyths.description: Dainsleif represents the destructive nature of unchecked ambition and the futility of endless conflict. Its curse serves as a cautionary tale against the pursuit of vengeance and the dangers of being consumed by hatred.

keyMyths.title: Dainsleif as a Symbol of Fate
keyMyths.description: Dainsleif can be interpreted as a symbol of inescapable fate, highlighting the Norse belief in predetermined destiny. Once drawn, the sword's effect is inevitable, mirroring the perceived powerlessness of individuals against the forces of fate.
